food_subgroup: Sweet soup dessert (chè) with sago pearls and coconut milk

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of banana sago dessert is approximately 250 g, containing banana pieces, sago pearls, and coconut milk broth.

nutrient_source.title: Chè Chuối (Banana Sweet Soup) — component-based estimation using HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for banana, sago, coconut milk, and sugar
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for banana sago (chè chuối) found in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values estimated from a weighted recipe model: approximately 35% banana (ripe), 15% sago pearls (cooked), 40% coconut milk (canned, diluted), 8% sugar, 2% pandan/salt. Weighted per-100g values derived from HPB Foodo entries for banana and coconut milk desserts and USDA SR Legacy entries for sago (tapioca pearls, cooked) and banana (raw). This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: Banana (ripe) GI value and coconut milk dessert analogy — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for banana sago (chè chuối) as a composite dish. GI estimated at 52 based on: ripe banana GI ~51 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008), cooked sago pearls GI ~66 (University of Sydney GI Database), and coconut milk fat content which lowers the overall GI of the dish. Weighted composite estimate yields approximately 52, classified as low GI (≤55). Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (52 × 17.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 23.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~38 mg/100g); potassium from banana supports blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: Banana sago is low in sodium (~95 mg per 250 g serving) and provides potassium (~363 mg per serving) from banana, making it a suitable occasional dessert for individuals managing hypertension, provided added salt is minimised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~23 g per serving) driven by ripe banana and sago pearls; coconut milk fat partially attenuates postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic load of approximately 23 per 250 g serving, banana sago should be consumed in small portions by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the coconut milk fat content moderates the glycemic response, but the high sugar and refined sago content warrant portion control and infrequent consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (~4.5 g/100g, ~11.3 g per serving) is the primary cardiovascular concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content from coconut milk (~11 g per 250 g serving) exceeds approximately 50% of the recommended daily saturated fat limit for cardiovascular health; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption of this dessert.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Banana Sago, known in Vietnamese as Chè Chuối, is a traditional Southeast Asian sweet soup dessert widely popular in Vietnam and served in Vietnamese restaurants across Singapore. The dish is prepared by simmering ripe banana pieces and cooked sago pearls (small translucent tapioca balls) in sweetened coconut milk, often flavoured with pandan leaf and a pinch of salt to balance the sweetness. The result is a warm or room-temperature dessert with a creamy, lightly fragrant coconut broth, soft banana pieces, and chewy sago pearls that provide textural contrast. The flavour profile is sweet, mildly tropical, and aromatic. Nutritionally, a standard 250 g bowl provides approximately 295 kcal, with the energy derived primarily from carbohydrates (banana and sago) and fat (coconut milk). The dish contains moderate saturated fat (~11 g per serving) from coconut milk, which is a consideration for cardiovascular health. The glycemic load is moderate (~23 per serving), driven by ripe banana and sago pearls, though the fat content of coconut milk partially attenuates the postprandial glucose response. Banana contributes potassium, vitamin B6, and vitamin C. The dish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, and halal. It is commonly served as a dessert or snack and is a staple of Vietnamese chè (sweet soup) culture, with the Singapore version often sold at Vietnamese restaurants and hawker stalls.

Is banana sago suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for banana sago (chè chuối) was found in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted recipe model using approved source data for individual components (banana, sago, coconut milk, sugar). GI is an analogous estimate based on component GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the recipe proportions are well-established for this dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates; no single approved-source entry for chè chuối as a composite dish was identified. Values should be updated if HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN publishes a direct entry. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — coconut milk and banana contain no industrially produced trans fats; naturally occurring trans fats in coconut are negligible and reported as 0 in USDA SR Legacy). 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — all ingredients are plant-based; USDA SR Legacy reports 0 mg cholesterol for banana, sago, and coconut milk). 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — this is an entirely plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is not present). 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no vitamin D-containing ingredients; USDA SR Legacy reports 0 for all components). 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.2), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1) are reported from USDA SR Legacy coconut milk and banana fatty acid profiles. 7. GI of 52 is an analogous composite estimate; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 8. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (52 × 17.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 23.14, rounded to 23.2. 9. The dish is served at Co Chung Restaurant - Lorong Telok on field survey data at SGD 8.98. 10. Vietnamese local name 'Chè Chuối' is included in local_names.vietnamese as this dish is of Vietnamese origin, even though the cuisine context is Singapore.
